vocabulary.explanation: A person employed by a tech platform to review and remove harmful, violent, or policy-violating material uploaded by users. The protagonist's job title and the film's central lens on trauma and complicity.

A cultural myth and real fear referring to a film that allegedly captures an actual murder for viewing pleasure. The concept is central to the horror of this film's premise and has roots in underground cinema folklore.

A British colloquial term from the 1980s used to describe banned or censored horror and exploitation films distributed on VHS, often depicting extreme violence. The original 'Faces of Death' was one of the most infamous video nasties.

The controversial pseudo-documentary cult film that inspired this 2026 remake. Originally directed by John Alan Schwartz, it claimed to show real footage of deaths but was largely staged. It remains one of the most notorious exploitation films ever made.

Also called vicarious trauma, it refers to the psychological damage caused by repeated exposure to accounts or images of others' suffering — a recognized occupational hazard for content moderators, first responders, and journalists.

The practice of deriving pleasure or fascination from watching others, especially without their knowledge or consent. The film uses this concept to critique both its characters and the audience watching the horror unfold.

A horror filmmaking style that presents content as if it were discovered, amateur recordings — e.g., The Blair Witch Project (1999). The violent videos the protagonist encounters in the film likely employ this aesthetic to heighten realism and dread.

Slang for the phenomenon of being led deeper and deeper into increasingly extreme online content by automated recommendation systems. Reflects the film's theme of how platforms pull users toward disturbing material.
Australian actor best known for playing Billy Hargrove in Netflix's Stranger Things. His casting signals a genre-savvy, prestige-adjacent tone for the film.

American actress and model known for her role as Kat Hernandez in HBO's Euphoria. Her presence connects the film to a generation of media that engages critically with body image, digital identity, and trauma.

The psychological process by which repeated exposure to disturbing stimuli reduces emotional response over time. A key theme of the film — both for the protagonist as a moderator and for audiences of violent media broadly.

A category of low-budget cinema that 'exploits' sensational content — sex, violence, taboo subjects — for commercial gain. The original Faces of Death belongs to this tradition, and the 2026 film critically engages with that legacy.

background.summary: Faces of Death (2026) is a psychological horror film directed by Daniel Goldhaber. It follows a woman working as a content moderator for a website who begins encountering a disturbing series of violent videos that appear to recreate death scenes from a notorious cult film. As she digs deeper into the footage, the line between her professional detachment and personal terror begins to collapse, raising unsettling questions about voyeurism, trauma, and the ethics of digital consumption.
context: The film draws its title and inspiration from the original 'Faces of Death' (1978), a controversial American pseudo-documentary that purported to show real footage of human and animal deaths. It became one of the most notorious 'video nasties' of its era.

Content moderation is a real and psychologically taxing profession in which workers are paid to review and remove harmful or violent content from online platforms. Studies have shown moderators frequently suffer from PTSD and secondary trauma.
The film explores themes of digital voyeurism — the act of passively watching disturbing content online — and implicates both the characters and the audience in the ethics of consuming violent media.

The horror subgenre of 'found footage' and 'snuff film' mythology serves as a cultural backdrop, tapping into longstanding fears about whether real death is being filmed and circulated on the internet.

The casting of Dacre Montgomery (Stranger Things), Barbie Ferreira (Euphoria), and Josie Totah brings a young, culturally visible ensemble associated with prestige genre television, signaling the film's appeal to a modern, socially conscious horror audience.
background.director_note: Daniel Goldhaber is an American filmmaker best known for directing Cam (2018), a critically acclaimed psychological horror film produced by Blumhouse and distributed by Netflix. Cam followed a webcam performer who discovers a doppelgänger has stolen her online identity — a premise that similarly interrogated surveillance, digital identity, and the commodification of the self online. Goldhaber's style is defined by a clinical yet deeply unsettling visual language: he favors tight framing, screen-mediated perspectives (showing action through phone screens, webcams, and monitors), and slow-burn tension over cheap jump scares. He is also known for How to Blow Up a Pipeline (2022), a gripping eco-thriller that demonstrated his ability to build ideological and political urgency into genre filmmaking. Across his work, Goldhaber consistently uses genre conventions as a lens to examine contemporary anxieties — about technology, identity, labor, and institutional power. With Faces of Death, he returns to his signature territory: the horror of what screens show us, and what watching does to the watcher.
background.wikipedia: Faces of Death is a 1978 American mondo horror film written and directed by John Alan Schwartz, credited under the pseudonyms "Conan Le Cilaire" and "Alan Black" respectively.
